item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another necropost, but I agree. It seems clear there was no malicious intent, but the fact is that many of these infomercial products are attempts to market mobility aids to the general public, and knowing that makes this a very uncomfortable read. The title especially bothers me—many if not most disabled people have been told their disabilities aren't really an issue and they just need to learn how to cope without the tools they need. Often, they're even told specifically that they only think they're disabled because some entity with some ulterior motive, such as the media or the medical establishment, has told them they are in order to make money.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last part killed it for me. Being ambiguous is not valid as an excuse for not providing even a hint. That's ambiguousness done wrong. You can even try the "Valid use of [Redacted]" test: You don't explicitly know what's going on? Yes. Do you have enough data to point very few possibilities intended by the author? No. Can the thing not said be any kind of ridiculous bullshit? Hell yeah!
The skip loses all momentum and falls flat on its face. Downvoted

item.title: For two easy payments...
item.content:encoded: It's a small concern, I know, but isn't that phrase ('seen-on-tv) rendered as "As Seen on TV"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this is terrifying.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very old thread, but since I'm downvoting for the same reason I think it's worth concluding.

Most of these seemingly ridiculous As Seen On TV products are designed and marketed for people with serious physical disabilities. To try to improve the marketing appeal, able-bodied actors use exagerated clumsiness to mimic the effects of those disabilities in a way that seems goofy to the average viewer, but instantly recognizable to the target audience. It's an ill-conceived dog whistle of sorts.

The article doesn't seem aware of that subtext, and while it doesn't show any ill intent, it's still well into uncomfortable territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My worst nightmare is turning into one of those guys on the infomercials that can't even computer, cook, eat, ect. Thank you for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrifying and I regret reading it. +1.

My theory on the undocumented facilities
- Just a theory...

The Foundation needs to contain some cognitohazardous broadcasts. They find the source, but going in obviously means risking exposure, and people who've been exposed can't be immediately identified- it takes days or weeks before symptoms show up. The only possibly safe thing to do is to seize the facility (and interrogate the operators, then either amnesticize or imprison them) and then have all personnel on site take an amnestic themselves.

And then they stop reporting back to base. Either they've forgotten how because of the amnestics, they've forgotten how because of the anomalous effect, or they've reasoned that because they're here to contain a cognitohazard whose spread they don't fully understand, it makes sense not to transmit anything because it might turn out to be a vector.

At this point they're kind of in limbo, operationally- they have the items, the transmission equipment, and the facility, but they can't move any of it and they can't call home for advice. They fall back on contain-in-place: set up a self-sufficient base camp (including amnestic production facilities, either for anyone who has to enter society for supplies or just for regular inoculations) and keep anything anomalous from getting out.

After that, 2407 starts happening to them. We know that the main symptom is forgetting how to do things, but from the interview log we also know that it can make you remember how to do things- the subject forgot how to use the Sham-Wow, and couldn't remember on their own, but then remembered when they saw the commercial again.

The containment personnel are already kind of helpless. They take care of themselves, but that's about it- their primary job is to contain this stuff, which amounts to not much, since nobody is trying to take it and it can't get away on its own. And they're holed up in an unused broadcast station. They probably watch a lot of TV.

They are now part of the target audience. "Feeling helpless? Use these convenient tools!"

And you know what convenient tools they're currently surrounded by, that the retrieved subjects aren't? Broadcasting equipment. And a soundstage. And everything else you need to start selling things by mail-order, including a warehouse full of prepackaged stuff for sale.

I'd bet that at some point when the broadcasts weren't coming out of that particular facility, there was a point when quite a lot of them were going in.

I'd also bet that if you searched that soundstage, you'd find at least one Mr. Microphone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
….

I just got this….wow…I wonder if they see things all in black and white just like they do in those infomercials…wow. Here's a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say the thought of getting dementia (which is pretty close to what this article is about) is pretty scary, yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew someone had to be buying them…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying anything about real disabilities. Disabilities are not even part of the conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a case of ambiguousness harming an article.

So, there are three elements that I see. First, it's an uncontained, poorly understood, and contagious SCP. That's a stylistic choice that takes some amount of effort and intentional restraint to maintain. Here, I think it lacks a bit of a sense of urgency and makes the content a little less punchy than it might be.

Second, you're extracting horror from infomercial behavior. I think you're fairly successful there despite the cheesy roots of the idea, so that worked out.

Then you have by far the most interesting element to me, the mysterious facilities. There are a bunch of possibilities for this.

just a few
– hide block

From least to most interesting, for example, you could have:
1) Foundation incompetence or unwieldiness
2) Some kind of interdimensional crossover
3) Someone impersonating the Foundation and abandoning their warehouses for… reasons
4) The Foundation having created SCP-2407 for some reason
5) The former containment of 2407 having gone horribly wrong, somehow leading to loss of records and the current state of 2407


Unfortunately, you don't give any indication which of these ideas might be plausible. There's no evidence to support or even suggest any particular theory. Instead of this allowing me to use my imagination to fill in the gap, this honestly just doesn't give me anything to sink my teeth into. The final result is that I just don't care, which is sad for such an interesting gambit.

Ultimately, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you're saying it's "real disabilities are scary"?

Downvote stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This ambiguity, put next to the anomaly's exploitation of certain personality flaws and disorders, kind of put me off. I've seen patients that become heavily dependent on fetishes or totems of sorts, and this reminded me of such situations. As such, it's not poorly handled… It's just off-putting for me.

So, no-vote for now. I'll revisit the doc, I swear. (dat false positive tho)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to say that in my headcanon, this skip is also brought to you by the fine folks behind SCP-2030 and SCP-449. They're getting better with the English.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Why do we have infomercials dedicated to those gimmick products, showing the people performing those basic tasks so ludicrously? Is it because we're moving into a world where marketers want to create a buzzer that alerts you to your eight glasses of water per day? Most people do fine without that. But the implication is that you need that product to hydrate yourself properly. What if we really did need to be reminded to drink water? That is the horror behind this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, this is what I took from it. Marketing for these products is simple: create a problem that doesn't exist. That's where you get all these infomercial clips of people seemingly unable to do completely simple tasks like hold a pencil or flip a pancake. Then the product swoops in and saves them from the plight of such impossible feats. It's the reason this exists.

I thought it was an excellent idea. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean if you're looking at it that way, literally any cognitohazard or SCP that causes people to not be able to function normally is an 'anomalous disability'. It's not about who the products are useful for irl, it's more about the perception about them created by the marketing, and how those silly clumsy people on TV could really be tragic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"people have anomalous disability causing them to use products which are typically marketed extremely badly but often actually super useful for people with disabilities"

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's supposed to be ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And suddenly the incompetent people on the infomercials seem so much more sympathetic and now induce a sensation of underlying horror. Well done. Well done, indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll take ten!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was surprisingly touched by the people losing motor skills and the ability to do things. Was not expecting that out of this premise. That was the article's main selling point for me, not sure how much it won me over.

Monitoring should continue until it is verified that SCP-2407 no longer poses a threat to that subject.

Does this mean the subjects die? (You don't mention anyone recovering from it.)

I'm confused as to the Foundation's possible involvement here. (Confusion in and of itself not a bad thing, but it seems like I'm supposed to understand it.) Were they manufacturing the products and also the ads as well? (SCP is behind the shamwow?) Are the amnestic devices supposed to be as-seen-on-TV products? Why would shipping the products and producing ads help contain them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that one of them is a false positive! What a touch of versimilitude!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ya done good, Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to my collab partner TroyL for making this go from a vague concept I had at work to the real deal, and bonus points for qntm for sending me on the train of thought that led to the formation of the initial concept.

If you order now, we'll throw what we forgot, free!
